title: Active Pulmonary Tuberculosis (TB) in 42-Year-Old Man with 4 Weeks of Productive Cough + Hemoptysis + Drenching Night Sweats + 6 kg Unintentional Weight Loss + Low-Grade Fevers: AFB Smear-Positive Sputum + Cavitary Right Upper Lobe Infiltrate on Chest X-Ray; Immediate AIRBORNE Isolation in Negative-Pressure Room + N95/PAPR Respiratory Protection + Initiation of RIPE Four-Drug Therapy (Rifampin + Isoniazid + Pyrazinamide + Ethambutol), Directly Observed Therapy (DOT), Mandatory Public Health Reporting, and Contact Investigation
subtitle: ACTIVE PULMONARY TUBERCULOSIS = airborne infection caused by MYCOBACTERIUM TUBERCULOSIS (acid-fast bacillus); TRANSMISSION via AIRBORNE DROPLET NUCLEI (1-5 micron particles that remain suspended in air for hours + travel on air currents) generated when person with active pulmonary/laryngeal TB coughs/sneezes/sings/speaks; NOT spread by fomites/surfaces/sharing utensils/touching (unlike contact-spread organisms) — this distinction drives isolation type; PATHOGENESIS = inhalation → alveolar macrophage ingestion → most contained as LATENT TB INFECTION (LTBI — granuloma + dormant bacilli + NOT infectious + asymptomatic + positive TST/IGRA + normal CXR or only calcified granuloma); REACTIVATION (or progressive primary) → ACTIVE TB (infectious + symptomatic + abnormal CXR); LIFETIME reactivation risk ~5-10% for immunocompetent + much higher with HIV/immunosuppression/TNF-inhibitors/diabetes/silicosis/renal failure/malnutrition; RISK FACTORS for exposure/infection = birth or residence in high-incidence country (Asia + Africa + Eastern Europe + Latin America) + close contact with active case + congregate settings (homeless shelters + correctional facilities + long-term care + healthcare) + healthcare workers; CLASSIC SYMPTOMS of active pulmonary TB = COUGH >2-3 weeks (initially dry then productive) + HEMOPTYSIS + drenching NIGHT SWEATS + unintentional WEIGHT LOSS + anorexia + fatigue + low-grade FEVER + pleuritic chest pain; EXTRAPULMONARY TB (lymphatic + pleural + skeletal/Pott disease + genitourinary + miliary/disseminated + meningeal + pericardial) more common with HIV. DIAGNOSIS: (1) AFB SPUTUM SMEAR (3 specimens collected 8-24 hr apart, ≥1 early-morning; acid-fast stain = rapid + indicates burden + infectiousness but does not distinguish M. tuberculosis from non-tuberculous mycobacteria); (2) NUCLEIC ACID AMPLIFICATION TEST (NAAT — e.g., Xpert MTB/RIF; rapid + confirms M. tuberculosis + detects rifampin resistance); (3) MYCOBACTERIAL CULTURE (gold standard + most sensitive + takes 2-6 weeks + provides drug-susceptibility testing — essential for resistance); (4) CHEST X-RAY (classic = upper lobe infiltrate + CAVITATION + reactivation pattern; primary TB = lower/middle lobe + hilar adenopathy + effusion; miliary = diffuse millet-seed nodules); (5) TST (tuberculin skin test — Mantoux PPD intradermal + read 48-72 hr + induration measured not erythema; cutoffs 5/10/15 mm by risk; indicates infection NOT active disease) OR IGRA (interferon-gamma release assay — QuantiFERON/T-SPOT; blood test + not affected by prior BCG vaccination + indicates infection NOT active disease); LATENT vs ACTIVE distinction critical (LTBI = positive TST/IGRA + asymptomatic + normal CXR + non-infectious + treat to prevent reactivation; ACTIVE = symptoms + abnormal CXR + positive smear/culture + infectious + treat to cure + isolate). INFECTION CONTROL: (1) AIRBORNE PRECAUTIONS immediately for suspected/confirmed active pulmonary or laryngeal TB — AIRBORNE INFECTION ISOLATION ROOM (AIIR) = NEGATIVE PRESSURE relative to hallway (air flows INTO room) + minimum 6-12 air changes per hour (ACH) + air exhausted directly outside OR through HEPA filtration before recirculation + door kept CLOSED; (2) RESPIRATORY PROTECTION = fit-tested N95 RESPIRATOR (or PAPR — powered air-purifying respirator) for all staff/visitors entering room; SURGICAL MASK is NOT adequate respiratory protection for staff (surgical mask filters large droplets not droplet nuclei) — surgical mask is placed on the PATIENT when transport is unavoidable (source control) + N95 protects the wearer; (3) MINIMIZE TRANSPORT — keep patient in room; if transport essential, patient wears SURGICAL MASK + schedule to minimize exposure; (4) PATIENT EDUCATION on cough etiquette + covering mouth + tissue disposal; (5) staff TB screening + post-exposure follow-up. PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT (drug-susceptible active TB): RIPE four-drug regimen for INTENSIVE PHASE × 2 months = RIFAMPIN (R; turns body fluids ORANGE-RED — urine/sweat/tears/contact lenses + induces hepatic CYP450 → many drug interactions including reduced oral contraceptive + warfarin + methadone efficacy + hepatotoxicity) + ISONIAZID (INH/H; hepatotoxicity + PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY prevented with PYRIDOXINE/vitamin B6 supplementation + drug-induced lupus) + PYRAZINAMIDE (PZA/Z; hepatotoxicity + HYPERURICEMIA/gout flare) + ETHAMBUTOL (EMB/E; OPTIC NEURITIS → monitor visual acuity + red-green color discrimination; renally cleared); followed by CONTINUATION PHASE × 4 months = RIFAMPIN + ISONIAZID (total 6 months for drug-susceptible pulmonary TB; longer for cavitary + positive 2-month culture + extrapulmonary + drug resistance); DRUG-RESISTANT TB (MDR-TB = resistant to ≥ INH + rifampin; XDR-TB = additionally resistant to fluoroquinolone + injectable/bedaquiline) requires prolonged multi-drug regimens (bedaquiline + linezolid + fluoroquinolones + others) + expert consultation. MONITORING: baseline + periodic LFTs (all three of INH/RIF/PZA hepatotoxic — hold if ALT >3× ULN with symptoms or >5× ULN asymptomatic) + visual acuity/color (ethambutol) + uric acid + adherence; DIRECTLY OBSERVED THERAPY (DOT) = standard of care to ensure adherence + prevent resistance + public health worker watches patient swallow each dose. PUBLIC HEALTH: TB is a MANDATORY REPORTABLE DISEASE — notify local/state public health department; CONTACT INVESTIGATION (identify + test + treat close contacts — concentric-circle approach starting with closest/highest-exposure); DOT coordination; INFECTIOUSNESS resolves after ~2 weeks of effective therapy + clinical improvement + 3 consecutive negative AFB smears (criteria for releasing from airborne isolation/discharge home with continued therapy). HEALTH PROMOTION: screening high-risk populations + LTBI treatment to prevent active disease + immigrant/refugee screening + congregate-setting screening + healthcare worker screening + BCG vaccine context (used in high-incidence countries; limited efficacy in adults; causes false-positive TST → use IGRA) + community education + social determinants (housing + nutrition + access). CONSEQUENCES of undiagnosed/untreated active TB = ongoing transmission (each untreated case infects ~10-15 contacts/year) + progressive lung destruction + respiratory failure + dissemination + death (untreated active TB mortality ~50%); drug resistance from non-adherence/inadequate regimens

patient.name: R. Okafor
patient.code: Full code; decisional capacity intact; goals = diagnosis + treatment + cure + protect family/community + return to work + maintain housing/employment during treatment
patient.admitDate: Presented to ED today after 4 WEEKS of progressively worsening PRODUCTIVE COUGH (now with streaks of BLOOD/HEMOPTYSIS × 5 days) + drenching NIGHT SWEATS (changing bedclothes nightly × 3 weeks) + 6 kg (13 lb) UNINTENTIONAL WEIGHT LOSS over 2 months + anorexia + fatigue + intermittent LOW-GRADE FEVERS + pleuritic right-sided chest pain; initially attributed cough to a 'lingering cold' + did not seek care due to work + no primary care + insurance concerns; presented today after coughing up visible blood frightened him; TRIAGE nurse noted chronic cough + hemoptysis + weight loss + night sweats + risk factors → immediately placed SURGICAL MASK on patient + moved to AIRBORNE INFECTION ISOLATION ROOM (negative pressure) + activated TB workup; CHEST X-RAY = CAVITARY infiltrate right upper lobe + fibronodular changes; AFB sputum smear collection initiated; admitted for airborne isolation + diagnostic confirmation + treatment initiation + public health coordination
patient.room: Airborne Infection Isolation Room (AIIR) — single negative-pressure room with anteroom; ≥6-12 air changes/hr + HEPA exhaust + door closed + N95/PAPR required to enter; signage posted; infectious disease + pulmonology + public health + social work + pharmacy multidisciplinary engagement

vitals.temp: 38.1°C (low-grade fever); alert + oriented + thin + ill-appearing + cachectic; productive cough with blood-streaked sputum during triage; SURGICAL MASK placed on patient immediately + escorted to AIRBORNE ISOLATION negative-pressure room; weight 58 kg (BMI 19; down from 64 kg 2 months ago); chest auscultation = coarse crackles right upper lung field + bronchial breath sounds; no respiratory distress at rest but tachypneic with exertion/coughing

vitals.time: 0700 hospital day 1 (after admission to AIIR + initial workup)
vitals.temp: 37.9°C; alert + oriented + cooperative; in AIRBORNE ISOLATION negative-pressure room + door closed + staff entering with fit-tested N95; CHEST X-RAY confirmed = CAVITARY infiltrate RIGHT UPPER LOBE + fibronodular opacities + no effusion; AFB sputum smears ×3 being collected 8-24 hr apart (first smear POSITIVE for acid-fast bacilli); NAAT (Xpert MTB/RIF) sent = POSITIVE for M. tuberculosis complex + NO rifampin resistance detected; mycobacterial culture + drug-susceptibility pending (2-6 wk); HIV test offered + sent (result pending); baseline LFTs + CBC + BMP + uric acid + visual acuity/color vision (pre-ethambutol) obtained

vitals.time: 1000 hospital day 1 (RIPE therapy initiated + public health notified)
vitals.temp: 37.6°C; RIPE FOUR-DRUG THERAPY initiated (rifampin + isoniazid + pyrazinamide + ethambutol) + PYRIDOXINE (vitamin B6) added to prevent isoniazid peripheral neuropathy; DIRECTLY OBSERVED THERAPY (DOT) arranged with public health; TB reported to local PUBLIC HEALTH DEPARTMENT (mandatory reportable disease) at 0930; CONTACT INVESTIGATION initiated (household = wife + 2 children + elderly mother + close work contacts); patient educated on rifampin turning body fluids ORANGE-RED (urine/sweat/tears) + medication adherence + cough etiquette + airborne transmission + isolation rationale; remains in negative-pressure isolation; emotional support + interpreter offered (English-fluent) + social work for housing/employment/insurance

37.2°C (defervescing); tolerating RIPE therapy; appetite improving slightly; cough persists but hemoptysis resolved; remains in airborne isolation (still smear-positive + early in therapy + infectious); LFTs stable (baseline normal; monitoring for hepatotoxicity); HIV result NEGATIVE; education ongoing + family contacts being screened by public health (TST/IGRA + symptom screen + CXR); DOT plan finalized for outpatient continuation; social work securing housing support + sick leave documentation

36.9°C (afebrile × 4 days); clinically improving — cough decreasing + appetite returning + weight stabilizing + energy improving; on RIPE × 10 days + clinical improvement documented; serial AFB smears showing declining bacillary load; pending 3 consecutive NEGATIVE AFB smears + ≥2 weeks effective therapy + clinical improvement for release from airborne isolation; LFTs remain stable; visual acuity/color vision normal (ethambutol monitoring); DOT + public health discharge coordination + contact investigation results returning (wife TST-positive → LTBI workup; children + mother being evaluated)

36.8°C (afebrile); clinically well-improved + on effective RIPE therapy >2 weeks + clinical improvement + 3 consecutive negative AFB smears achieved → criteria met for release from airborne isolation + discharge home with continued therapy + DOT; comprehensive education completed (6-month regimen + intensive phase 2 mo RIPE then continuation 4 mo RIF+INH + adherence + side effects + LFT monitoring + visual monitoring + rifampin orange fluids + drug interactions + follow-up); public health DOT + monthly clinic follow-up + contact investigation ongoing (wife on LTBI treatment; children + mother cleared or under evaluation); social work secured housing + employment accommodation + insurance enrollment

labs.v: 42 y/o male R. Okafor presenting to ED after 4 WEEKS of progressively worsening PRODUCTIVE COUGH (now with HEMOPTYSIS — blood-streaked sputum × 5 days) + drenching NIGHT SWEATS (changing bedclothes nightly × 3 weeks) + 6 kg (13 lb) UNINTENTIONAL WEIGHT LOSS over 2 months + anorexia + fatigue + intermittent LOW-GRADE FEVERS + pleuritic right-sided chest pain. RISK FACTORS: emigrated from Nigeria (high TB-incidence country) 3 years ago; works in a busy restaurant kitchen (congregate work setting); shares a 2-bedroom apartment with wife + 2 young children (ages 6 + 9) + elderly mother (68); no prior TB diagnosis or treatment; received BCG vaccine in childhood (per common practice in country of origin); no known TB contacts that he is aware of; no HIV history (HIV status unknown — to be tested). PMH: generally healthy; no chronic conditions; no immunosuppression; not on TNF-inhibitors/steroids; no diabetes (will screen); occasional alcohol use (social); never smoker. SOCIAL: married 12 years; wife works part-time + cares for children + mother; 2 children in elementary school; elderly mother has HTN + lives with family; recent immigrant + working to establish stability; no primary care physician + uninsured (work does not provide insurance) + has avoided care due to cost + work obligations; English-fluent (also speaks Igbo) + interpreter offered but not needed; Christian faith + church community + chaplain available; financially strained + concerned about missing work + housing + supporting family during illness. PRESENTATION COURSE: initially attributed cough to a 'lingering cold' + did not seek care due to work + cost + no primary care; HEMOPTYSIS × 5 days frightened him → presented to ED today. TRIAGE recognition: nurse noted CHRONIC COUGH >3 weeks + HEMOPTYSIS + WEIGHT LOSS + NIGHT SWEATS + recent immigration from high-incidence country = HIGH SUSPICION FOR ACTIVE PULMONARY TB → IMMEDIATELY placed SURGICAL MASK on patient (source control) + escorted to AIRBORNE INFECTION ISOLATION ROOM (negative pressure) + activated TB workup + notified provider + minimized waiting-room/congregate exposure. ED VITALS: HR 104 + BP 118/74 + RR 22 + SpO2 94% RA + T 38.1°C; thin + ill-appearing + cachectic; weight 58 kg (BMI 19; down from 64 kg); chest = coarse crackles right upper lung + bronchial breath sounds; tachypneic with exertion/coughing; no acute respiratory distress at rest. CHEST X-RAY = CAVITARY infiltrate RIGHT UPPER LOBE + fibronodular changes (classic reactivation TB pattern).
labs.r: CLASSIC ACTIVE PULMONARY TB presentation — chronic productive cough + hemoptysis + night sweats + weight loss + low-grade fever + high-incidence country origin + congregate living/work. RAPID TRIAGE RECOGNITION + immediate source control (surgical mask on patient) + AIRBORNE ISOLATION prevented further exposure. RN role: recognition + isolation initiation + escalation + protect others.

labs.n: TB diagnostics + latent vs active distinction
labs.v: TB DIAGNOSTIC WORKUP: (1) AFB SPUTUM SMEAR — 3 sputum specimens collected 8-24 hr apart with ≥1 EARLY-MORNING specimen; acid-fast stain (Ziehl-Neelsen or auramine-rhodamine fluorescence); RAPID + indicates bacillary burden + correlates with INFECTIOUSNESS (smear-positive = more infectious); does NOT distinguish M. tuberculosis from non-tuberculous mycobacteria (NTM); this patient FIRST SMEAR POSITIVE for AFB. (2) NUCLEIC ACID AMPLIFICATION TEST (NAAT — Xpert MTB/RIF) — rapid (hours) + confirms M. tuberculosis complex + simultaneously detects RIFAMPIN RESISTANCE (marker for MDR-TB); this patient NAAT POSITIVE for M. tuberculosis + NO rifampin resistance (supports drug-susceptible TB + standard RIPE regimen). (3) MYCOBACTERIAL CULTURE — GOLD STANDARD + most sensitive + grows in 2-6 weeks (slow-growing organism) + provides definitive DRUG-SUSCEPTIBILITY TESTING (essential to confirm regimen + detect resistance); pending. (4) CHEST X-RAY — this patient CAVITARY right upper lobe (classic REACTIVATION TB; upper lobe predilection due to high oxygen tension; cavitation = high bacillary burden + more infectious); primary TB pattern = lower/middle lobe + hilar lymphadenopathy + effusion; MILIARY TB = diffuse millet-seed micronodules (disseminated). (5) HIV TESTING — recommended for all TB patients (HIV co-infection common + alters presentation + treatment + immune reconstitution); this patient HIV NEGATIVE. (6) BASELINE LABS before therapy = LFTs (hepatotoxic drugs) + CBC + BMP + uric acid (pyrazinamide) + visual acuity + red-green color discrimination (ethambutol baseline). LATENT TB INFECTION (LTBI) vs ACTIVE TB DISTINCTION — CRITICAL: LTBI = positive TST or IGRA + ASYMPTOMATIC + NORMAL CXR (or calcified granuloma) + NON-INFECTIOUS + dormant bacilli contained by immune system + treat to PREVENT reactivation (isoniazid × 9 mo OR rifampin × 4 mo OR INH+rifapentine weekly × 12 wk/3HP); ACTIVE TB = SYMPTOMATIC + ABNORMAL CXR + positive smear/NAAT/culture + INFECTIOUS + treat to CURE (multidrug RIPE) + ISOLATE. TST (Mantoux PPD) = 0.1 mL intradermal forearm + read 48-72 hr + measure INDURATION (not erythema) in mm; cutoffs by risk: ≥5 mm positive (HIV + recent contact + immunosuppressed + fibrotic CXR), ≥10 mm (recent immigrant from high-incidence country + IVDU + congregate-setting resident/worker + healthcare worker + children + high-risk medical conditions), ≥15 mm (no risk factors); indicates INFECTION not active disease; BCG vaccine causes FALSE-POSITIVE TST → prefer IGRA. IGRA (QuantiFERON-TB Gold + T-SPOT.TB) = blood test measuring interferon-gamma release by T-cells to TB antigens; NOT affected by prior BCG vaccination (preferred for BCG-vaccinated like this patient + his family) + single visit; indicates INFECTION not active disease.
labs.r: DIAGNOSIS = AFB smear (rapid + infectiousness) + NAAT (confirms M. tuberculosis + rifampin resistance) + culture (gold standard + drug susceptibility) + CXR (cavitary RUL = reactivation) + HIV test. LATENT (positive TST/IGRA + asymptomatic + normal CXR + non-infectious) vs ACTIVE (symptomatic + abnormal CXR + positive smear + infectious) distinction drives isolation + treatment. RN role: specimen collection + result interpretation + education.

labs.n: AIRBORNE precautions + respiratory protection + isolation engineering
labs.v: AIRBORNE PRECAUTIONS (Transmission-Based Precautions for organisms spread by airborne droplet nuclei — TB + measles/rubeola + varicella/disseminated zoster; distinct from DROPLET precautions for influenza/pertussis/meningococcus + CONTACT precautions for MDRO/C. diff): (1) AIRBORNE INFECTION ISOLATION ROOM (AIIR) = engineering controls — NEGATIVE PRESSURE relative to surrounding areas (air flows INTO the room from the hallway so contaminated air does not escape; verify with daily smoke-tube or monitor check) + minimum 6 air changes per hour (ACH) for existing + 12 ACH for new construction + air EXHAUSTED DIRECTLY to the OUTSIDE away from intake vents OR recirculated only through HEPA FILTRATION + DOOR KEPT CLOSED at all times (open door defeats negative pressure) + anteroom preferred; (2) RESPIRATORY PROTECTION (personal protective equipment) — staff and visitors entering wear a FIT-TESTED N95 RESPIRATOR or higher (N99/N100 or PAPR — powered air-purifying respirator for those who cannot be fit-tested e.g., facial hair, or for high-risk aerosol-generating procedures); N95 must be FIT-TESTED + seal-checked each use; a SURGICAL/ISOLATION MASK is NOT adequate respiratory protection for staff because it does not seal and does not filter 1-5 micron droplet nuclei — surgical masks are for large-droplet protection + source control; (3) SOURCE CONTROL — the PATIENT wears a SURGICAL MASK (not N95) whenever they must leave the AIIR (e.g., essential transport/imaging) — the surgical mask on the patient contains their respiratory secretions/droplet nuclei at the source; patient does NOT wear an N95 (N95 is for protecting the wearer from inhaling + harder to breathe through + unnecessary for source control); (4) MINIMIZE TRANSPORT — perform procedures in the room when possible; if transport essential, notify receiving department + schedule to limit others' exposure + patient masked + transport staff in N95; (5) AEROSOL-GENERATING PROCEDURES (sputum induction + bronchoscopy + intubation + nebulizers) increase risk → perform in AIIR with N95/PAPR + minimize personnel; (6) PATIENT EDUCATION — cough etiquette + cover mouth/nose with tissue + dispose + hand hygiene + understanding of isolation rationale to support adherence + reduce isolation distress; (7) VISITOR management — limit + instruct on N95 + screening; (8) HEALTHCARE WORKER protection — annual TB screening + baseline + post-exposure testing + fit-testing program + symptom awareness; (9) DURATION — maintain airborne isolation until criteria for non-infectiousness met (effective therapy ≥2 weeks + clinical improvement + 3 consecutive negative AFB smears) — then may release from isolation/discharge with continued therapy. CONTRAST with WRONG approaches: standard/droplet precautions alone are INADEQUATE for TB; placing patient in a positive-pressure room (e.g., protective environment for immunocompromised) would PUSH contaminated air OUT into the hallway = dangerous; relying on surgical masks for staff = inadequate filtration; leaving the door open defeats negative pressure.
labs.r: AIRBORNE PRECAUTIONS = NEGATIVE-PRESSURE AIIR (6-12 ACH + HEPA/exterior exhaust + door closed) + FIT-TESTED N95/PAPR for staff + SURGICAL MASK on PATIENT for source control during essential transport + minimize transport + cough etiquette + HCW screening. NOT droplet/contact precautions; NOT positive pressure; surgical mask NOT adequate for staff. RN role: enforce engineering + PPE + education + protect self/others.

labs.n: RIPE therapy + adverse effects + monitoring + DOT
labs.v: PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT of drug-susceptible active pulmonary TB = RIPE four-drug regimen. INTENSIVE PHASE × 2 MONTHS = all four drugs (R+I+P+E): RIFAMPIN (R) — bactericidal + sterilizing; turns BODY FLUIDS ORANGE-RED (urine + sweat + tears + saliva — stains soft CONTACT LENSES permanently + harmless but counsel patient to expect it = adherence/reassurance); potent HEPATIC CYP450 INDUCER → MANY DRUG INTERACTIONS — reduces efficacy of ORAL CONTRACEPTIVES (advise backup/non-hormonal contraception) + WARFARIN (reduced anticoagulation) + METHADONE (precipitates withdrawal) + many antiretrovirals/azoles/etc.; HEPATOTOXICITY + flu-like syndrome + thrombocytopenia. ISONIAZID (INH/H) — bactericidal; HEPATOTOXICITY (age-related risk + alcohol increases) + PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (pyridoxine-responsive — give PYRIDOXINE/vitamin B6 25-50 mg daily to prevent, especially in malnutrition/diabetes/alcohol/pregnancy/HIV) + drug-induced lupus + can elevate phenytoin levels. PYRAZINAMIDE (PZA/Z) — sterilizing in acidic environment; HEPATOTOXICITY + HYPERURICEMIA (can precipitate gout; benign arthralgias common) + GI upset; avoid/caution in gout + significant liver disease. ETHAMBUTOL (EMB/E) — bacteriostatic; principal toxicity is OPTIC NEURITIS (dose/duration-related — decreased visual acuity + RED-GREEN COLOR DISCRIMINATION loss + central scotoma; usually reversible if caught early → BASELINE + monthly visual acuity + color vision; instruct patient to report any vision change immediately); renally cleared → adjust in renal impairment. PYRIDOXINE (vitamin B6) added with isoniazid. CONTINUATION PHASE × 4 MONTHS = RIFAMPIN + ISONIAZID (drop PZA + EMB after intensive phase if susceptible) → TOTAL 6 MONTHS for drug-susceptible pulmonary TB; EXTEND (9+ months) for cavitary disease with positive 2-month culture + extrapulmonary (bone/CNS longer) + slow response + drug resistance. MONITORING: baseline + periodic LFTs (INH + RIF + PZA all hepatotoxic — symptomatic monitoring + check LFTs; HOLD hepatotoxic drugs if ALT/AST >3× ULN WITH symptoms (nausea/RUQ pain/jaundice/dark urine) OR >5× ULN asymptomatic + reintroduce sequentially); baseline + monthly visual acuity/color (ethambutol); uric acid if symptomatic (pyrazinamide); monthly clinical assessment + sputum smear/culture (document conversion to negative = treatment response; positive culture at 2 months → extend therapy); adherence assessment. DIRECTLY OBSERVED THERAPY (DOT) = STANDARD OF CARE — a healthcare/public-health worker directly WATCHES the patient swallow each dose (in person or via video DOT); ensures adherence + completion + prevents acquired DRUG RESISTANCE (resistance arises from incomplete/erratic therapy) + tracks side effects + supports patient; especially important given 6-month duration + asymptomatic improvement leading to premature discontinuation risk. DRUG-RESISTANT TB: MDR-TB (resistant to ≥ isoniazid + rifampin — the two most potent drugs) + XDR-TB (additionally resistant to fluoroquinolones + bedaquiline/linezolid) require prolonged individualized regimens (bedaquiline + linezolid + fluoroquinolone + others 18-20+ months) + expert/CDC consultation + longer isolation + worse outcomes — prevention via adherence + DOT + susceptibility-guided therapy.
labs.r: RIPE = Rifampin (orange fluids + CYP450 inducer + many drug interactions + hepatotox) + Isoniazid (hepatotox + peripheral neuropathy → give pyridoxine/B6) + Pyrazinamide (hepatotox + hyperuricemia) + Ethambutol (optic neuritis → monitor visual acuity + color); intensive 2 mo (4 drugs) then continuation 4 mo (RIF+INH) = 6 mo total. Monitor LFTs + vision. DOT = standard of care to ensure adherence + prevent resistance. RN role: administration + monitoring + adherence + education.

labs.n: Public health reporting + contact investigation + isolation release + Health Promotion
labs.v: PUBLIC HEALTH responsibilities (Safety & Infection Control + Health Promotion intersect): (1) MANDATORY REPORTING — TUBERCULOSIS is a NATIONALLY NOTIFIABLE/REPORTABLE communicable disease; the diagnosing facility/provider MUST report suspected + confirmed cases to the LOCAL/STATE PUBLIC HEALTH DEPARTMENT (typically within 24 hr to 1 business day depending on jurisdiction) — this is a legal/ethical duty that overrides individual privacy for communicable-disease control (HIPAA permits disclosure to public health authorities); reporting triggers public-health case management + resources. (2) CONTACT INVESTIGATION — public health conducts a CONCENTRIC-CIRCLE/'stone-in-the-pond' investigation starting with the CLOSEST + HIGHEST-EXPOSURE contacts (household members + close work contacts) then expanding if transmission found; each contact is evaluated with symptom screen + TST or IGRA + chest X-ray if indicated; goals = find secondary active cases (test + treat + isolate) + identify recently infected contacts for LTBI treatment to prevent progression; this patient's HOUSEHOLD (wife + 2 children ages 6 + 9 + elderly mother 68) + close restaurant work contacts prioritized; CHILDREN + ELDERLY + immunocompromised contacts are highest priority (higher progression risk); window-period retesting at 8-10 weeks for initially negative close contacts. (3) DOT coordination — public health arranges directly observed therapy + adherence support + side-effect monitoring + completion tracking. (4) ISOLATION RELEASE / NON-INFECTIOUSNESS criteria — a hospitalized patient may be released from AIRBORNE ISOLATION (and discharged home to complete therapy) when ALL met: (a) received effective standard multidrug therapy for ≥2 weeks; (b) demonstrated CLINICAL IMPROVEMENT (reduced cough + afebrile + improving); (c) 3 CONSECUTIVE NEGATIVE AFB SPUTUM SMEARS from sputum collected on separate days; for DISCHARGE HOME during the infectious period (if household already exposed + no high-risk susceptible new contacts + able to adhere + home isolation feasible) public health makes individualized determination; this patient met criteria by hospital day 14. (5) DISCHARGE planning — continued RIPE via DOT + monthly clinic follow-up + LFT/vision monitoring + completion of full 6-month course + home isolation guidance until non-infectious + return-to-work clearance from public health. HEALTH PROMOTION + PREVENTION: (a) SCREENING high-risk populations — recent immigrants/refugees from high-incidence countries + close contacts + congregate-setting residents/workers (correctional + homeless shelter + long-term care) + healthcare workers + immunosuppressed/HIV + diabetics — with TST/IGRA; (b) LTBI TREATMENT — treating latent infection prevents progression to active disease (the key prevention strategy — e.g., this patient's contacts found to have LTBI); (c) BCG VACCINE context — given at birth in high-incidence countries; limited + variable efficacy against adult pulmonary TB (better at preventing severe childhood/miliary/meningeal TB); causes false-positive TST → use IGRA in BCG-vaccinated; not routinely used in low-incidence countries; (d) ADDRESSING SOCIAL DETERMINANTS — housing + nutrition + healthcare access + insurance + employment protection during treatment + reducing stigma + culturally appropriate education + interpreter services; (e) COMMUNITY EDUCATION + de-stigmatization to encourage early care-seeking. ETHICAL/LEGAL nuance: balancing individual liberty/privacy vs public health; voluntary adherence preferred + supported by DOT + social support; legal isolation orders/detention reserved for rare non-adherent infectious cases posing public danger (last resort + due process).
labs.r: TB is MANDATORY REPORTABLE → notify public health (legal duty + HIPAA-permitted). CONTACT INVESTIGATION (concentric circles; household + close contacts; children/elderly/immunocompromised priority) + DOT + LTBI treatment of infected contacts. ISOLATION RELEASE = ≥2 wk effective therapy + clinical improvement + 3 negative AFB smears. HEALTH PROMOTION = screen high-risk + treat LTBI + address social determinants + community education. RN role: reporting + coordination + education + advocacy.

labs.v: PERTINENT NEGATIVES: HIV NEGATIVE (no co-infection — simplifies treatment + better prognosis + no immune reconstitution concerns); NO rifampin resistance on NAAT (supports drug-SUSCEPTIBLE TB + standard RIPE regimen — not MDR-TB); NO miliary/disseminated TB (localized cavitary RUL — no diffuse micronodular pattern + no meningeal/CNS signs + no hepatosplenomegaly suggesting dissemination); NO TB meningitis (no headache + no neck stiffness + no altered mental status + no focal neuro deficit); NO massive hemoptysis (blood-streaked sputum only — not life-threatening pulmonary hemorrhage requiring emergent bronchial artery embolization); NO respiratory failure (SpO2 94-98% + no severe distress + no need for high-flow/ventilation); NO hepatotoxicity at baseline (normal baseline LFTs — safe to start hepatotoxic regimen with monitoring); NO baseline visual deficit (normal acuity/color — safe ethambutol baseline); NO gout/significant hyperuricemia precluding pyrazinamide; NO diabetes (screened — would increase progression + complicate); NO immunosuppressive medications (no steroids/TNF-inhibitors); NO drug allergies (NKDA). PROTECTIVE FACTORS: RAPID TRIAGE RECOGNITION of chronic cough + hemoptysis + weight loss + night sweats + high-incidence-country origin → immediate source control (surgical mask on patient) + AIRBORNE ISOLATION prevented further transmission in the ED/waiting area; PROMPT DIAGNOSIS (smear + NAAT confirmed M. tuberculosis + susceptibility-supportive within day 1); DRUG-SUSCEPTIBLE organism (standard 6-month RIPE highly curative — >95% cure with adherence); HIV-negative + immunocompetent (better prognosis + lower progression risk in contacts); EARLY EFFECTIVE THERAPY → rapid clinical improvement + defervescence + smear conversion; DIRECTLY OBSERVED THERAPY (DOT) arranged ensuring adherence + preventing resistance; PUBLIC HEALTH engagement + contact investigation protecting family + community; STRONG FAMILY + faith/church community support; MOTIVATED patient + good comprehension + English-fluent + interpreter available; SOCIAL WORK securing housing + employment accommodation + insurance enrollment (addressing social determinants that drive non-adherence); FAMILY contacts being screened + treated for LTBI (preventing future active cases); NO drug resistance + no complications. EXPECTED trajectory with comprehensive intervention = clinical cure with full 6-month adherent therapy + protection of contacts + return to work + maintained housing/family stability + de-stigmatized care.
labs.r: Multiple PROTECTIVE FACTORS support cure + community protection: rapid recognition + isolation + prompt diagnosis + drug-susceptible organism + HIV-negative + early effective therapy + DOT + public health contact investigation + strong family/community + social work addressing determinants. Standard expected clinical cure with adherent 6-month therapy + protected contacts.

nursesNote: ED 1015 — 42 y/o male R. Okafor presented after 4 WEEKS of progressively worsening PRODUCTIVE COUGH (now with HEMOPTYSIS — blood-streaked sputum × 5 days) + drenching NIGHT SWEATS (changing bedclothes nightly × 3 weeks) + 6 kg (13 lb) UNINTENTIONAL WEIGHT LOSS over 2 months + anorexia + fatigue + intermittent LOW-GRADE FEVERS + pleuritic right chest pain. RISK FACTORS: emigrated from Nigeria (high TB-incidence country) 3 yr ago + works in busy restaurant kitchen (congregate setting) + shares 2-bedroom apartment with wife + 2 children (6 + 9) + elderly mother (68); BCG-vaccinated childhood; no prior TB dx/treatment; no known TB contacts; HIV status unknown (to test); uninsured + no primary care + avoided care due to cost + work. PMH: healthy + no immunosuppression + no diabetes + occasional alcohol + never smoker. SOCIAL: married 12 yr + wife part-time work + cares for children/mother + 2 elementary-age children + elderly mother with HTN; recent immigrant + financially strained + concerned about work/housing/family during illness; English-fluent (also Igbo) + interpreter offered not needed; Christian + church community + chaplain available. TRIAGE RECOGNITION: nurse noted CHRONIC COUGH >3 wk + HEMOPTYSIS + WEIGHT LOSS + NIGHT SWEATS + recent immigration from high-incidence country = HIGH SUSPICION ACTIVE PULMONARY TB → IMMEDIATELY placed SURGICAL MASK on PATIENT (source control) + escorted to AIRBORNE INFECTION ISOLATION ROOM (negative pressure) + activated TB workup + notified provider + minimized waiting-room/congregate exposure. ED VITALS: HR 104 + BP 118/74 + RR 22 + SpO2 94% RA + T 38.1°C; thin + ill-appearing + cachectic; weight 58 kg (BMI 19; down from 64 kg); chest = coarse crackles RUL + bronchial breath sounds; tachypneic with exertion/coughing; no acute distress at rest. CHEST X-RAY = CAVITARY infiltrate RIGHT UPPER LOBE + fibronodular changes (classic reactivation TB). DAY 1 0700 (admitted to AIIR): in AIRBORNE ISOLATION negative-pressure room + door CLOSED + staff entering with FIT-TESTED N95; AFB sputum smears ×3 being collected 8-24 hr apart with early-morning specimen (FIRST SMEAR POSITIVE for acid-fast bacilli); NAAT (Xpert MTB/RIF) POSITIVE for M. tuberculosis complex + NO rifampin resistance; mycobacterial culture + drug-susceptibility pending (2-6 wk); HIV test sent; baseline LFTs + CBC + BMP + uric acid + visual acuity + red-green color discrimination (pre-ethambutol) obtained — all baseline acceptable. DAY 1 1000 (treatment + reporting): RIPE FOUR-DRUG THERAPY initiated — RIFAMPIN + ISONIAZID + PYRAZINAMIDE + ETHAMBUTOL + PYRIDOXINE (vitamin B6) added to prevent isoniazid peripheral neuropathy; DIRECTLY OBSERVED THERAPY (DOT) arranged with public health; TB REPORTED to LOCAL PUBLIC HEALTH DEPARTMENT (mandatory reportable disease) at 0930; CONTACT INVESTIGATION initiated (household = wife + 2 children + elderly mother + close work contacts; children + elderly prioritized). EDUCATION: rifampin turns body fluids ORANGE-RED (urine/sweat/tears + stains contact lenses — expected + harmless) + medication adherence + 6-month duration + cough etiquette + airborne transmission + isolation rationale + report vision changes (ethambutol) + report nausea/RUQ pain/jaundice/dark urine (hepatotoxicity) + backup contraception counseling relayed to wife (rifampin reduces OCP efficacy); emotional support + social work for housing/employment/insurance + chaplain offered. DAY 3: T defervescing 37.2 + tolerating RIPE + appetite improving + hemoptysis resolved + cough persists; remains in airborne isolation (still smear-positive + early therapy + infectious); LFTs stable; HIV NEGATIVE; family contacts being screened by public health (TST/IGRA + symptom screen + CXR); DOT plan finalized; social work securing housing support + sick-leave documentation. DAY 10: afebrile × 4 days + clinically improving (cough decreasing + appetite returning + weight stabilizing + energy improving); on RIPE × 10 days; serial AFB smears showing declining bacillary load; pending 3 consecutive NEGATIVE smears + ≥2 wk effective therapy + clinical improvement for isolation release; LFTs stable + visual acuity/color normal; contact results returning (wife TST-positive → LTBI workup; children + mother under evaluation). DAY 14: clinically well-improved + effective RIPE >2 weeks + clinical improvement + 3 CONSECUTIVE NEGATIVE AFB SMEARS achieved → ISOLATION RELEASE criteria met + DISCHARGE HOME with continued therapy via DOT; comprehensive education completed (intensive phase 2 mo RIPE then continuation 4 mo RIF+INH = 6 mo total + adherence + side effects + LFT/vision monitoring + rifampin orange fluids + drug interactions + follow-up + home isolation guidance until cleared); public health DOT + monthly clinic follow-up + contact investigation ongoing (wife on LTBI treatment; children + mother cleared/under evaluation); social work secured housing + employment accommodation + insurance enrollment. DISCHARGE: outpatient TB clinic monthly + public health DOT + complete full 6-month course + return-to-work clearance from public health + family/community + faith support + crisis resources + emergency contacts.
providerOrders: ADMIT to Airborne Infection Isolation Room (AIIR) — negative-pressure single room
DIAGNOSIS: active pulmonary tuberculosis (AFB smear-positive + NAAT-positive M. tuberculosis + cavitary RUL infiltrate); rule out drug resistance (susceptibility pending); HIV-negative
CODE STATUS: FULL CODE; decisional capacity intact
ISOLATION — AIRBORNE PRECAUTIONS (initiate immediately + maintain until non-infectious criteria met):
Negative-pressure AIIR + minimum 6-12 air changes/hr + HEPA/exterior exhaust + DOOR CLOSED at all times
Verify negative pressure each shift (smoke test/monitor) + document
FIT-TESTED N95 respirator (or PAPR) required for ALL staff/visitors entering — seal-check each use
SURGICAL MASK on PATIENT for source control during any essential transport (NOT N95 on patient)
Minimize transport — perform procedures in room when possible; notify receiving dept + schedule to limit exposure if transport essential
Aerosol-generating procedures (sputum induction/bronchoscopy/nebulizer) in AIIR with N95/PAPR + minimal personnel
Airborne precautions signage on door + cough etiquette education
DIAGNOSTIC WORKUP:
AFB sputum smear ×3 — collect 8-24 hr apart, ≥1 early-morning specimen
NAAT (Xpert MTB/RIF) — confirm M. tuberculosis + rifampin-resistance screen (DONE: positive, no rif resistance)
Mycobacterial culture + drug-susceptibility testing (gold standard; 2-6 wk; pending)
Chest X-ray (DONE: cavitary RUL + fibronodular)
HIV test (DONE: negative)
Baseline LFTs (AST/ALT/bilirubin), CBC, BMP, uric acid
Baseline visual acuity + red-green color discrimination (pre-ethambutol)
Screen for diabetes (HbA1c/glucose); hepatitis B/C if indicated
PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY — RIPE regimen (drug-susceptible active pulmonary TB):
INTENSIVE PHASE × 2 months (all four):
RIFAMPIN — counsel: orange-red body fluids (urine/sweat/tears; stains contact lenses); CYP450 inducer (reduces OCP/warfarin/methadone efficacy → backup contraception); monitor hepatotoxicity
ISONIAZID (INH) — monitor hepatotoxicity + peripheral neuropathy
PYRAZINAMIDE (PZA) — monitor hepatotoxicity + hyperuricemia/gout
ETHAMBUTOL (EMB) — monitor for optic neuritis (visual acuity + red-green color); renal dose-adjust
PYRIDOXINE (vitamin B6) 25-50 mg daily — prevent isoniazid peripheral neuropathy
CONTINUATION PHASE × 4 months: RIFAMPIN + ISONIAZID (total 6 months; extend if cavitary + positive 2-mo culture)
DIRECTLY OBSERVED THERAPY (DOT) — public health coordinated; observe each dose; ensures adherence + prevents resistance
MONITORING:
LFTs at baseline + periodically (hold hepatotoxic drugs if ALT/AST >3× ULN with symptoms or >5× ULN asymptomatic; report nausea/RUQ pain/jaundice/dark urine)
Monthly visual acuity + color discrimination (ethambutol)
Monthly clinical assessment + sputum smear/culture (document conversion; positive 2-mo culture → extend therapy)
Uric acid if gout symptoms (pyrazinamide)
Weight + nutrition (cachexia; dietitian consult)
Vital signs q4h; SpO2; respiratory assessment
PUBLIC HEALTH:
REPORT to local/state public health department — mandatory reportable disease (DONE 0930 day 1)

CONTACT INVESTIGATION — household (wife + 2 children + elderly mother) + close work contacts; concentric-circle; children/elderly prioritized; TST/IGRA + symptom screen + CXR; window-period retest 8-10 wk
Coordinate DOT + adherence support + outpatient TB clinic follow-up
ISOLATION RELEASE / discharge criteria: ≥2 weeks effective therapy + clinical improvement + 3 consecutive negative AFB smears
MULTIDISCIPLINARY + supportive:
Infectious disease + pulmonology consultation
Public health TB program (reporting + contact investigation + DOT)
Pharmacy (regimen + interactions + counseling + adherence)
Social work — housing + employment accommodation/sick leave + insurance enrollment + financial assistance + addressing social determinants
Dietitian — nutritional repletion (cachexia/weight loss)
Chaplain — faith/church community support (offered)
Interpreter services available (English-fluent; Igbo offered)
PATIENT + FAMILY EDUCATION + TEACH-BACK:
TB transmission (airborne droplet nuclei; cough etiquette; isolation rationale) + reduce stigma
6-month regimen + importance of completion + DOT + consequences of non-adherence (resistance/relapse)
Side effects to report: vision changes (ethambutol); nausea/RUQ pain/jaundice/dark urine (hepatotoxicity); numbness/tingling (neuropathy)
Rifampin orange-red body fluids expected + harmless; contact-lens staining; backup contraception (reduced OCP efficacy)
Home isolation guidance until public health clears as non-infectious
Family contact testing/treatment (LTBI prevention) + protecting children/elderly mother
Follow-up: monthly TB clinic + public health DOT + return-to-work clearance
DISCHARGE PLANNING (when non-infectious criteria met — day 14):
Discharge home with continued RIPE via DOT
Monthly outpatient TB clinic + LFT/vision monitoring + sputum conversion tracking
Public health case management + contact investigation completion
Social work follow-up (housing + employment + insurance)
Crisis/symptom resources + emergency contacts + when to return (massive hemoptysis + severe dyspnea + jaundice + vision loss)

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters at this active TB presentation?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring nursing attention.

opts.text: CLASSIC ACTIVE PULMONARY TB symptom cluster — productive COUGH >3 weeks + HEMOPTYSIS + drenching NIGHT SWEATS + unintentional WEIGHT LOSS (6 kg) + anorexia + fatigue + LOW-GRADE FEVER; plus high-incidence-country origin (Nigeria) + congregate work/living = HIGH SUSPICION for active pulmonary TB
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — classic active TB presentation + epidemiologic risk. Triggers airborne isolation + workup. RN role: recognition + escalation.

opts.text: IMMEDIATE source control + AIRBORNE ISOLATION at recognition — place SURGICAL MASK on the PATIENT + move to a NEGATIVE-PRESSURE airborne infection isolation room (AIIR) + door closed + minimize waiting-room/congregate exposure; do not wait for confirmation to isolate a suspected case
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — suspected infectious TB requires immediate airborne isolation + source control to protect others. RN role: initiate isolation + protect patients/staff.

opts.text: RESPIRATORY PROTECTION for staff = FIT-TESTED N95 respirator (or PAPR) to enter the room; a surgical mask is NOT adequate respiratory protection for staff (does not filter droplet nuclei) — surgical mask goes on the PATIENT for source control during essential transport
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — correct PPE distinction (N95 on staff; surgical mask on patient). RN role: enforce respiratory protection + protect self/others.

opts.text: DIAGNOSTIC confirmation — AFB sputum smear ×3 (8-24 hr apart + early-morning; indicates infectiousness) + NAAT/Xpert MTB-RIF (confirms M. tuberculosis + rifampin resistance) + mycobacterial culture (gold standard + drug susceptibility) + cavitary RUL on CXR (reactivation pattern) + HIV test
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — diagnostic workup confirms active TB + guides therapy + assesses resistance/infectiousness. RN role: specimen collection + coordination.

opts.text: RIPE four-drug therapy initiation — Rifampin + Isoniazid + Pyrazinamide + Ethambutol + pyridoxine (B6); intensive phase 2 mo then continuation RIF+INH 4 mo = 6 mo total; with DIRECTLY OBSERVED THERAPY (DOT) to ensure adherence + prevent resistance
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — standard treatment of drug-susceptible active TB + DOT. RN role: administration + monitoring + adherence + education.

opts.text: Adverse-effect monitoring — LFTs (rifampin + isoniazid + pyrazinamide hepatotoxic) + visual acuity/red-green color (ethambutol optic neuritis) + peripheral neuropathy (isoniazid → pyridoxine) + uric acid (pyrazinamide); counsel rifampin turns body fluids orange-red + reduces oral contraceptive efficacy
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — RIPE drug toxicities + counseling. RN role: monitoring + education + early detection of adverse effects.

opts.text: PUBLIC HEALTH duties — TB is a MANDATORY REPORTABLE disease → notify public health department; CONTACT INVESTIGATION of household (wife + 2 children + elderly mother) + close work contacts (children/elderly prioritized) for testing + LTBI treatment
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — mandatory reporting + contact investigation protect family + community. RN role: reporting + coordination + advocacy.

opts.text: Social determinants + support — uninsured + no primary care + financial strain + housing + employment concerns + recent immigrant + family caregiver roles; social work + interpreter availability + faith/community support are essential to adherence + completion
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — addressing social determinants drives adherence + completion + prevents resistance/relapse. RN role: social work referral + holistic support.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation. No medication contraindications. Not the priority cue but important for safety.

opts.text: Place the patient on CONTACT precautions with gown + gloves in a standard positive-pressure room; TB spreads by touching contaminated surfaces so surface disinfection is the priority
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. TB spreads by AIRBORNE droplet nuclei, NOT contact/fomites. Contact precautions + standard room are INADEQUATE; a POSITIVE-pressure room pushes contaminated air OUT into the hallway. Correct = AIRBORNE precautions in NEGATIVE-pressure AIIR + N95.

opts.text: A surgical mask on staff entering the room provides adequate protection against TB; N95 fit-testing is unnecessary and overly cautious
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. A surgical mask does NOT filter 1-5 micron droplet nuclei + does not seal → inadequate staff protection against TB. Staff require FIT-TESTED N95 or PAPR. The surgical mask is for the PATIENT (source control).

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What TB transmission + isolation + treatment principles apply?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles.

opts.text: TB TRANSMISSION = AIRBORNE droplet nuclei (1-5 micron particles suspended in air for hours) generated by coughing/sneezing/speaking by a person with active pulmonary/laryngeal TB; requires AIRBORNE precautions (negative-pressure AIIR + N95) — distinct from droplet (influenza/pertussis) + contact (MDRO/C. diff) precautions
opts.rat: CORRECT — airborne transmission principle drives isolation type. Foundation for infection control.

opts.text: LATENT TB (positive TST/IGRA + asymptomatic + normal CXR + NON-infectious + dormant bacilli; treat to prevent reactivation) vs ACTIVE TB (symptomatic + abnormal/cavitary CXR + positive smear/NAAT/culture + INFECTIOUS; treat to cure + isolate) — this patient has ACTIVE pulmonary TB
opts.rat: CORRECT — latent vs active distinction drives isolation + treatment. Foundation for management + contact investigation.

opts.text: DIAGNOSTICS — AFB smear (rapid + correlates with infectiousness; does not distinguish species) + NAAT/Xpert (confirms M. tuberculosis + rifampin resistance) + culture (gold standard + drug susceptibility; 2-6 wk) + cavitary upper-lobe CXR (reactivation; high bacillary burden) + HIV test (co-infection alters management)
opts.rat: CORRECT — diagnostic framework. Foundation for confirmation + resistance assessment + treatment selection.

opts.text: RIPE pharmacology — Rifampin (orange fluids + CYP450 inducer + drug interactions + hepatotoxic) + Isoniazid (hepatotoxic + peripheral neuropathy → pyridoxine) + Pyrazinamide (hepatotoxic + hyperuricemia) + Ethambutol (optic neuritis → monitor vision); 2-month intensive (4 drugs) then 4-month continuation (RIF+INH) = 6 months; DOT prevents resistance
opts.rat: CORRECT — RIPE regimen + toxicities + duration + DOT. Foundation for treatment + monitoring + adherence.

opts.text: RESPIRATORY PROTECTION distinction — staff wear FIT-TESTED N95/PAPR (filters droplet nuclei + seals) to protect the wearer; the PATIENT wears a SURGICAL MASK during essential transport (source control — contains secretions at the source); a surgical mask is inadequate respiratory protection for staff
opts.rat: CORRECT — N95-for-staff vs surgical-mask-for-patient principle. Foundation for correct PPE practice.

opts.text: PUBLIC HEALTH — TB is mandatory reportable (legal duty + HIPAA permits disclosure to public health); contact investigation (concentric circles; household + close contacts; children/elderly/immunocompromised prioritized) + DOT + LTBI treatment of infected contacts prevents future active cases
opts.rat: CORRECT — public health reporting + contact investigation framework. Foundation for community protection + Health Promotion.

opts.text: ISOLATION RELEASE / non-infectiousness criteria = effective multidrug therapy ≥2 weeks + clinical improvement + 3 consecutive NEGATIVE AFB sputum smears; until then maintain airborne isolation (even if symptomatically improving)
opts.rat: CORRECT — isolation release criteria. Foundation for safe de-isolation/discharge timing.

opts.text: TB spreads primarily through contaminated surfaces + shared utensils, so meticulous environmental disinfection + dedicated dishware are the most important controls; respiratory protection is secondary
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. TB is AIRBORNE, not spread by fomites/utensils. Environmental disinfection + dedicated dishware are NOT the key controls. Airborne engineering controls (negative pressure) + respiratory protection (N95) are primary.

opts.text: Once the patient feels better and fever resolves (a few days), antibiotics can be stopped to avoid side effects; completing the full 6 months is unnecessary if symptoms resolve
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. Premature discontinuation causes RELAPSE + acquired DRUG RESISTANCE (MDR/XDR-TB). The FULL 6-month regimen must be completed (DOT supports this) regardless of symptom resolution.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns at this active TB presentation
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: AIRBORNE TRANSMISSION CONTROL r/t suspected/confirmed active pulmonary TB — immediate source control (surgical mask on patient) + NEGATIVE-PRESSURE AIIR + door closed + FIT-TESTED N95/PAPR for staff + minimize transport; protect patients/staff/community from airborne spread
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Preventing airborne transmission protects many. Time-critical at recognition. RN role: immediate isolation + PPE enforcement.

opts.text: DIAGNOSIS + EFFECTIVE THERAPY r/t AFB smear + NAAT + culture + CXR + HIV; initiate RIPE four-drug regimen + pyridoxine + DOT promptly — early effective therapy reduces infectiousness + cures disease + prevents complications/resistance
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Prompt diagnosis + effective therapy reduce infectiousness + cure. RN role: specimen collection + medication administration + monitoring.

opts.text: ADVERSE-EFFECT MONITORING + SAFETY r/t RIPE toxicities — LFTs (hepatotoxicity) + visual acuity/color (ethambutol) + neuropathy (isoniazid/pyridoxine) + uric acid (pyrazinamide); counsel rifampin orange fluids + drug interactions; early detection prevents harm
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Drug toxicity monitoring + counseling prevent serious harm + support adherence. RN role: monitoring + education + escalation.

opts.text: PUBLIC HEALTH REPORTING + CONTACT INVESTIGATION r/t mandatory reportable disease + household (wife + 2 children + elderly mother) + work contacts; protect family + community via testing + LTBI treatment + DOT coordination
opts.rat: #4 — Significant + time-sensitive. Reporting + contact investigation protect community + identify secondary cases. RN role: reporting + coordination + advocacy.

opts.text: SOCIAL DETERMINANTS + adherence support + holistic care r/t uninsured + financial strain + housing + employment + recent immigrant + family caregiver roles + faith/community; social work + interpreter + education support completion of the full 6-month course
opts.rat: #5 — Important throughout + for long-term success. Addressing determinants drives adherence + completion + prevents relapse/resistance. RN role: social work referral + holistic support + education.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this active pulmonary TB.

opts.text: Place patient in a NEGATIVE-PRESSURE airborne infection isolation room (AIIR) with ≥6-12 air changes/hr + HEPA/exterior exhaust + door kept closed; verify negative pressure each shift
opts.rat: Standard airborne precautions engineering control for active pulmonary TB. RN role: ensure room setup + verify negative pressure + keep door closed.

opts.text: Require all staff/visitors to wear a FIT-TESTED N95 respirator (or PAPR) to enter the room + seal-check each use
opts.rat: Standard respiratory protection for airborne TB. RN role: enforce N95/PAPR + fit-testing + seal checks.

opts.text: Place a SURGICAL MASK on the PATIENT for source control if essential transport outside the room is required + minimize transport
opts.rat: Standard source control during transport. Surgical mask on patient contains droplet nuclei at source. RN role: mask patient + coordinate/limit transport.

opts.text: Collect 3 AFB sputum specimens 8-24 hr apart (≥1 early-morning) + send NAAT + mycobacterial culture with drug-susceptibility testing + obtain HIV test + baseline LFTs/vision
opts.rat: Standard diagnostic workup. RN role: specimen collection + coordination + baseline labs.

opts.text: Initiate RIPE (rifampin + isoniazid + pyrazinamide + ethambutol) + pyridoxine (B6) with directly observed therapy (DOT); plan 2-month intensive + 4-month continuation (RIF+INH) = 6 months
opts.rat: Standard treatment of drug-susceptible active pulmonary TB + DOT. RN role: administration + monitoring + adherence + education.

opts.text: Monitor LFTs + visual acuity/red-green color discrimination + peripheral neuropathy + uric acid; counsel that rifampin turns body fluids orange-red + reduces oral contraceptive efficacy (advise backup contraception)
opts.rat: Standard RIPE toxicity monitoring + counseling. RN role: monitoring + education + early detection.

opts.text: Report the case to the public health department (mandatory reportable) + initiate contact investigation of household + close work contacts + coordinate DOT + outpatient follow-up
opts.rat: Standard public health duty + contact investigation. RN role: reporting + coordination + advocacy.

opts.text: Consult social work for housing/employment/insurance + dietitian for nutritional repletion + interpreter services + chaplain; provide education with teach-back to support adherence
opts.rat: Standard holistic + social-determinant support driving adherence + completion. RN role: referrals + holistic care + education.

opts.text: Use CONTACT precautions (gown + gloves) in a standard or positive-pressure room and rely on surface disinfection as the primary control
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. TB is airborne, not contact/fomite spread. Contact precautions/standard room inadequate; positive pressure pushes contaminated air into the hallway. Use airborne precautions + negative-pressure AIIR + N95.

opts.text: Discontinue all TB medications once the patient is afebrile and feeling better (a few days) to avoid drug side effects
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. Premature discontinuation causes relapse + acquired drug resistance (MDR/XDR-TB). Complete the FULL 6-month regimen via DOT regardless of symptom resolution.

opts.text: Give an N95 respirator to the patient to wear at all times and let staff enter wearing only a surgical mask
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG (reversed PPE). Staff need FIT-TESTED N95/PAPR (protect the wearer); the PATIENT wears a SURGICAL MASK for source control during transport. A surgical mask is inadequate respiratory protection for staff.

opts.text: Skip pyridoxine (vitamin B6) since it is not part of the RIPE acronym and adds unnecessary pills
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Pyridoxine (B6) is given WITH isoniazid to PREVENT peripheral neuropathy (especially in malnutrition/diabetes/alcohol/pregnancy/HIV). It is standard adjunct despite not being in the acronym.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: FIRST action sequence at suspected active TB
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the RN should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: IMMEDIATE source control + isolation — place a SURGICAL MASK on the patient + move to a NEGATIVE-PRESSURE AIIR + close the door + ensure staff don FIT-TESTED N95 before entering + minimize exposure of other patients/staff
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Stop airborne transmission immediately at recognition. RN role: source control + isolation + PPE.

opts.text: Collect AFB sputum specimens (×3, 8-24 hr apart) + send NAAT/culture + obtain CXR + HIV test + baseline LFTs/vision; notify provider + infectious disease
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Confirm diagnosis + assess resistance/infectiousness + baseline for therapy. RN role: specimen collection + coordination.

opts.text: Initiate RIPE + pyridoxine with DOT once ordered + educate on regimen/side effects (orange fluids, vision changes, hepatotoxicity, neuropathy) + counsel on adherence and the full 6-month course
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Effective therapy reduces infectiousness + cures + prevents resistance. RN role: administration + monitoring + education.

opts.text: Report to the public health department (mandatory) + initiate contact investigation of household (wife + 2 children + elderly mother) + close work contacts + coordinate DOT + outpatient follow-up
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Reporting + contact investigation protect family/community + find secondary cases. RN role: reporting + coordination.

opts.text: Engage social work (housing/employment/insurance) + dietitian + interpreter + chaplain + reinforce education with teach-back to support adherence + plan isolation release/discharge criteria
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing + foundational. Addressing social determinants + holistic support drive adherence + completion. RN role: referrals + holistic support + discharge planning.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Day 1: patient in NEGATIVE-PRESSURE AIIR + door closed + staff using fit-tested N95; AFB smear-positive + NAAT-positive M. tuberculosis with NO rifampin resistance; RIPE + pyridoxine initiated + DOT arranged + TB reported to public health + contact investigation started; HIV negative
opts.rat: Standard correct early management — isolation + diagnosis + drug-susceptible therapy + DOT + reporting + contact investigation. Foundation for cure + community protection.

opts.text: Day 3: afebrile-trending (37.2°C) + tolerating RIPE + appetite improving + hemoptysis resolved + cough persists; remains in airborne isolation (still smear-positive + early therapy); LFTs stable; family contacts being screened by public health
opts.rat: Standard expected early response to effective therapy with appropriate continued isolation + monitoring + contact investigation. Foundation for ongoing care.

opts.text: Day 10: afebrile × 4 days + cough decreasing + appetite returning + weight stabilizing + energy improving; serial AFB smears with declining bacillary load; LFTs stable + visual acuity/color normal; pending 3 negative smears for isolation release
opts.rat: Standard expected clinical improvement + smear conversion trend + stable toxicity monitoring. Foundation for isolation release/discharge planning.

opts.text: Day 14: clinically well + effective therapy >2 weeks + clinical improvement + 3 consecutive NEGATIVE AFB smears → isolation-release criteria met + discharge home with continued RIPE via DOT + monthly follow-up + contact investigation ongoing (wife on LTBI treatment)
opts.rat: Excellent trajectory — non-infectiousness criteria met + safe discharge with continued therapy + community protection + LTBI treatment of contact. Foundation for cure.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — staff entered the room wearing only surgical masks + the door was propped open for convenience; two weeks later a nurse and the patient's roommate's visitor convert to positive TST/IGRA, and the patient's untreated cavitary disease progressed before isolation was enforced
opts.rat: ⚠️ FAILURE of airborne controls (no N95 + door open defeating negative pressure) → transmission to staff/others. Demonstrates WHY fit-tested N95 + closed-door negative pressure matter. Triggers exposure investigation + RCA + system improvement.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — patient felt better by day 5 and a provider stopped all TB drugs to 'avoid side effects'; 3 months later he returns with recurrent cavitary disease + culture now shows isoniazid + rifampin resistance (MDR-TB) requiring prolonged second-line therapy + prolonged isolation
opts.rat: ⚠️ Premature discontinuation → relapse + acquired MDR-TB. Demonstrates why the full 6-month DOT-supported regimen is essential. Triggers expert/CDC consultation + prolonged complex therapy + RCA.

opts.text: Week 4 alternative — patient reports new blurred vision + difficulty distinguishing red from green; ethambutol-associated optic neuritis suspected; drug held + ophthalmology referral + regimen adjusted + vision monitored for recovery
opts.rat: ⚠️ Ethambutol optic neuritis (dose/duration-related; often reversible if caught early). Demonstrates importance of vision monitoring + prompt drug-hold + referral. Appropriate response prevents permanent vision loss.

opts.text: Month 2 outpatient via DOT — sputum culture converted to negative + weight regained + back at work (cleared by public health as non-infectious) + completing continuation phase (RIF+INH); wife completing LTBI treatment + children/mother cleared
opts.rat: Standard expected response — culture conversion + clinical recovery + return to work + contacts protected. Foundation for cure + community protection.

opts.text: Month 6 — completed full RIPE/continuation course via DOT + cured + culture-negative + asymptomatic + weight restored + working + housed; contact investigation complete (no secondary active cases; LTBI contacts treated); de-stigmatized + reintegrated with family/community
opts.rat: Excellent final outcome — completed adherent therapy → cure + protected contacts + restored function + addressed social determinants. Standard ideal trajectory for drug-susceptible TB.

opts.text: Week 6 alternative — patient reports nausea + RUQ pain + dark urine + scleral icterus; LFTs show ALT 6× ULN; drug-induced hepatotoxicity suspected; hepatotoxic drugs held + LFTs trended + regimen reintroduced sequentially once recovered per ID guidance
opts.rat: ⚠️ RIPE hepatotoxicity (rifampin + isoniazid + pyrazinamide). Demonstrates importance of LFT monitoring + symptom reporting + appropriate drug-hold/reintroduction. Appropriate response prevents fulminant liver injury.
